svg.title: Directory Structure, Protection, File System Implementation

section.h2: Directory Structure, Protection, File System Implementation
section.p: A file system is a critical component of an operating system, responsible for managing files and storage devices.

div.p: A directory is a file system structure that stores and organizes files and subdirectories.
A tree structure is a hierarchical representation of directories, where each directory is a node with subdirectories as children.

ul.li: Flat Directory Structure: A simple, linear structure with no nesting.
Hierarchical Directory Structure: A tree-like structure with directories as nodes.
Graph-based Directory Structure: A representation using nodes and edges, similar to a graph data structure.

section.h3: Advantages and Disadvantages of Directory Structures

tr.td: Scalable, efficient for large file systems

div.p: Protection is a mechanism that restricts access to sensitive data and resources.
Access control is a method of controlling access to files, directories, and other resources.

ul.li: Access Control: A method of controlling access to files, directories, and other resources.
File System Implementation: A mechanism that restricts access to sensitive data and resources.
Unix File System (UFS): A hierarchical directory structure with a tree-like organization.
NTFS (New Technology File System): A file system with a graph-based directory structure.
